matter.title: Roe v. Fairbanks et al. (Improvident Transfer of Title)
matter.summary: Acting through a conservator/next friend, Grace C. Roe, an elderly and dependent person, sues to void a deed of a shorefront cottage and lot to Maria Q. Fairbanks, a neighbor who became a caregiver, made for a nominal sum far below value without independent counsel, invoking the statutory presumption of undue influence under Maine's Improvident Transfers of Title Act.

plaintiff.role: Plaintiff (the elderly transferor, acting through a conservator/next friend)

defendant.role: Defendant transferee (caretaker / child / companion who received the deed)

defendant_2.role: Defendant (co-transferee or spouse of the transferee)

other_party.role: Interested adult child of the transferor

client.role: Plaintiff (the elderly transferor, acting through a conservator/next friend)

fact_pattern.summary: Acting through a conservator/next friend, Grace C. Roe, an elderly and dependent person, sues to void a deed of a shorefront cottage and lot to Maria Q. Fairbanks, a neighbor who became a caregiver, made for a nominal sum far below value without independent counsel, invoking the statutory presumption of undue influence under Maine's Improvident Transfers of Title Act.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe is 92 years old and, by the time of the transfer, is homebound and dependent after a stroke. Grace had come to rely on Maria Q. Fairbanks, a neighbor who became a caregiver, for day-to-day support, placing the two in a relationship of trust and confidence. Around the same period, Grace was living with cognitive decline after a stroke.

On or about 2024-08-11, Grace executed a deed conveying a shorefront cottage and lot to Maria Q. Fairbanks (joined by Ingrid B. Kingsley) in exchange for a nominal sum far below value, although the property was worth roughly $868656. Grace had no independent attorney advising Grace about the conveyance, and the transaction was arranged within the caretaking relationship rather than at arm's length.

The transfer came to light when Omar W. Loe, an adult child of the transferor, learned the home had been signed away. A conservator was sought to protect Grace, and this action followed in Sagadahoc County Superior Court. Because an elderly dependent person made a major transfer of real property, for less than full consideration, in a confidential relationship, and without independent counsel, the Improvident Transfers of Title Act presumes the transfer was the product of undue influence and the plaintiff asks the court to set the deed aside, impose a constructive trust, and return the property.
undisputed_facts: Grace C. Roe executed a deed conveying a shorefront cottage and lot to Maria Q. Fairbanks on or about 2024-08-11.
Grace C. Roe was an elderly person who, at the time of the deed, depended on others for care.
Grace C. Roe was not separately represented by an attorney of Grace's own in the transaction.
disputed_facts: Whether the deed is presumptively the product of undue influence and whether the transferee can rebut that presumption.
Whether Grace C. Roe had the mental capacity to understand and execute the conveyance when it was signed.
Whether the value of any care provided amounted to full and fair consideration for a shorefront cottage and lot.

timeline.event: Grace executed the deed conveying a shorefront cottage and lot to Maria Q. Fairbanks.
Complaint to void the transfer prepared for filing in Bath Superior Court.

third_parties.name: Samuel Q. Inglewood
third_parties.role: Attorney who drafted the deed, if any
third_parties.relevance: Prepared or recorded the deed and may bear on whether the transferor had any independent advice about the conveyance.

third_parties.name: John W. Poe
third_parties.role: Other adult child of the transferor
third_parties.relationship: Sibling of the transferee and child of the transferor
third_parties.relevance: An interested heir who was excluded from the transaction and supports setting the deed aside.

third_parties.name: Theodore S. Larkspur
third_parties.role: Notary and witnesses to the deed
third_parties.relevance: Notarized and witnessed the deed and can testify to the circumstances, the transferor's apparent condition, and who was present at signing.

third_parties.name: Wesley B. Poe
third_parties.relevance: Treated the transferor around the time of the deed and can speak to the transferor's cognition and capacity to understand and execute a conveyance.

exchanges.question: As conservator, what outcome are you seeking for the transferor?
exchanges.answer: I want the home put back. The deed should be undone so a shorefront cottage and lot returns to the person it belongs to, and I want to make sure nothing else is lost while we sort this out.

exchanges.question: What was the relationship between the transferor and the person who received the deed?
exchanges.answer: The transferee was a neighbor who became a caregiver. The transferor had really come to depend on them for everything day to day, so there was a lot of trust there, maybe too much.

exchanges.question: What do you understand about how the deed came about?
exchanges.answer: The transferor signed over a shorefront cottage and lot for a nominal sum far below value, even though it is worth a great deal. There was no lawyer on the transferor's side, and the family did not know until afterward.

exchanges.question: What was the transferor's condition around that time?
exchanges.answer: Around then the transferor was dealing with cognitive decline after a stroke, and is homebound and dependent after a stroke. I have real doubts they fully understood what signing the deed meant.

exchanges.question: How does the rest of the family see this?
exchanges.answer: Another of the children flagged it and is with me on this. We are not trying to start a feud, but the home should not have left this way.

exchanges.question: How do you feel about the cost and difficulty of litigation?
exchanges.answer: I am cautious. I am acting for a vulnerable person, so I want to be careful and protect what is left rather than gamble. Still, this needs to be put right.

goals: Void the deed and return a shorefront cottage and lot to the transferor and the transferor's estate.
Protect the transferor's remaining assets and benefits from further depletion.
Hold the transferee accountable for any improper benefit obtained through the transfer.
priorities: Recovering the home and undoing the improvident transfer.
Safeguarding the elder's dignity and remaining resources.
A clean, well-supported record establishing the statutory presumption.
desired_outcomes: Rescission of the deed and an order, or constructive trust, restoring the property to the transferor.
Preservation of the transferor's remaining accounts and benefits.
non_starters: Any resolution that leaves the home with the transferee or sacrifices the elder's security.

constraints.budget: Modest; the conservator will fund a capacity evaluation and an appraisal but wants costs kept reasonable.
constraints.timeline: Wants the property secured and the deed challenged promptly before the transferor's interests erode further.

issues.issue: Whether the transaction is a 'major transfer' by an 'elderly dependent person' within the statute
sub_questions: Does the conveyance of the home or farm qualify as a major transfer of real property?
Does the transferor meet the statutory definition of an elderly dependent person?
issues.client_position: The conveyance of the family real estate by a dependent elder squarely fits the statutory definitions.

issues.issue: Statutory presumption of undue influence on a major transfer by an elderly dependent person
sub_questions: Was the transferor an elderly dependent person who made a major transfer for less than full consideration?
Did a confidential or fiduciary relationship and the absence of independent counsel trigger the presumption of undue influence?
issues.client_position: The transfer satisfies every statutory element, so undue influence is presumed and the deed should be set aside.

issues.issue: Common-law lack of capacity to convey
issues.governing_law: Common law; capacity to execute a deed
sub_questions: Did the transferor understand the nature and effect of the conveyance when it was signed?
Does the medical and cognitive evidence show a lack of capacity?
issues.client_position: The transferor's cognitive condition left Grace without the capacity to make a knowing conveyance.

issues.issue: Timeliness of the action under the statute of limitations
sub_questions: Was the action brought within the statutory period?
Does the transferor's condition affect the limitations analysis?
issues.client_position: The action is timely under the statute governing improvident transfers.

issues.issue: The transferee's burden to rebut the presumption of undue influence
sub_questions: What must the transferee prove to rebut the presumption?
Can the transferee show the transfer was fair, voluntary, and well understood?
issues.client_position: The transferee cannot carry the burden of showing the transfer was fair, voluntary, and fully understood.

sub_questions: Did the transferee use the relationship of trust to overcome the transferor's free will?
Do the surrounding circumstances independently show undue influence?
issues.client_position: Apart from the statute, the transferee exploited a relationship of trust to procure the deed.

expert_opinions.topic: The transferor's capacity at the time of the transfer
expert_opinions.opinion: At the time of the deed, the transferor's cognitive condition impaired the capacity to understand and knowingly execute a conveyance of real property.
expert_opinions.basis: Clinical examination, cognitive testing, and review of the contemporaneous medical record.

expert_opinions.topic: Tracing the transferor's finances and the use of assets
expert_opinions.opinion: Account records show the transferor's resources were depleted in and around the period of the transfer.
expert_opinions.basis: Bank statements, ledgers, and transaction histories.

evidence.title: Transferor's medical and cognitive records
evidence.description: Medical records and cognitive evaluations bearing on the transferor's capacity around the time of the deed.

evidence.title: Transferor's financial and bank records
evidence.description: Bank and account records showing the transferor's finances and any depletion of assets.

evidence.title: Conservatorship and competency filings
evidence.description: The conservatorship petition and any competency or capacity filings concerning the transferor.

evidence.title: Appraisal showing value far above the consideration
evidence.description: Appraisal of the property's fair market value contrasted with the consideration actually paid.

amounts.label: Transferor's depleted accounts
amounts.basis: Remaining balances in the transferor's accounts.

amounts.label: Claimed value of care services offered as consideration
amounts.basis: Asserted value of care the transferee says was provided.

amounts.label: Estimated cost of cognitive and appraisal experts
amounts.basis: Retained capacity, valuation, and accounting experts.

causes_of_action.claim: Improvident transfer / statutory presumption of undue influence
elements: The transferor was an elderly dependent person
Who made a major transfer of property for less than full consideration
In a confidential or fiduciary relationship and without independent counsel
Triggering a presumption of undue influence the transferee must rebut
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Rescission of the deed and an order setting the transfer aside under 33 M.R.S. § 1023

causes_of_action.against: Maria Q. Fairbanks and Ingrid B. Kingsley
elements: A relationship of trust and confidence
Susceptibility of the transferor to influence
Opportunity and disposition to exert influence
A transfer that reflects the influence rather than the transferor's free will
causes_of_action.relief_sought: An order voiding the deed as the product of undue influence

causes_of_action.governing_law: Common law; capacity to execute a deed
elements: The transferor executed the deed
While lacking the mental capacity to understand its nature and effect
Due to the transferor's cognitive condition
Rendering the conveyance voidable
causes_of_action.relief_sought: A declaration that the deed is void for want of capacity

causes_of_action.against: Maria Q. Fairbanks and Ingrid B. Kingsley
elements: The transferee holds title obtained through an improvident transfer
Retention of the property would unjustly enrich the transferee
Equity requires the property be held for the transferor
A constructive trust is the appropriate remedy
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Imposition of a constructive trust and an order returning the property to the transferor

elements: The transferor owned personal property and funds
The transferee exercised dominion over them
Without authorization and inconsistent with the transferor's rights
Causing the transferor loss
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Damages for the value of personal property and funds converted

affirmative_defenses.defense: Full and fair consideration through years of care
affirmative_defenses.basis: Years of care and support furnished by the transferee were full and fair consideration for the transfer.
The transferor ratified and confirmed the conveyance after it was made.
The transferor competently and voluntarily intended the conveyance as a gift.
The transferor understood the nature and effect of the deed and was advised of the transaction. 33 M.R.S. § 1022.

counterclaims.claim: Quantum meruit for the value of care provided
counterclaims.relief: Compensation for the reasonable value of care and services furnished to the transferor if the deed is set aside.

discovery.description: Requests for financial records and signed medical authorizations for the transferor's records.
Subpoena to the bank for the transferor's account records.
Interrogatories on the circumstances of the transfer and the relationship of trust.

motions.title: Motion for a constructive trust and lis pendens
motions.relief: A constructive trust and a recorded lis pendens preserving the property pending judgment.

motions.title: Motion for appointment of a guardian ad litem
motions.relief: Appointment of a guardian ad litem to protect the transferor's interests.

motions.title: Motion to compel the transferor's medical records
motions.relief: An order compelling production of the transferor's medical and cognitive records.

docket.entry: Complaint to void the transfer and demand for relief filed.
Answer and counterclaim for the value of care filed.
Order appointing a guardian ad litem for the transferor entered.
Order compelling the transferor's medical records entered.
Court-ordered capacity evaluation completed and report served.
Hearing held on the motion establishing the statutory presumption.

facts.dependency_basis: is homebound and dependent after a stroke
facts.relationship_to_transferee: a neighbor who became a caregiver
facts.consideration_paid: a nominal sum far below value
